nodes.title: Unit 1: Set Theory
nodes.content: Foundational concepts of sets, subsets, power sets, and set operations.

nodes.title: Set and Cardinality
nodes.content: A set is a collection of objects. Cardinality |A| is the count of distinct elements.

nodes.title: Subsets and Proper Subsets
nodes.content: A is a subset of B if all elements of A are in B. Proper subsets must exclude at least one element of B.

nodes.title: Theorem: Cardinality of Power Sets
nodes.content: The power set P(A) contains all subsets of A. Its cardinality is |P(A)| = 2^|A|.

nodes.title: Union and Intersection
nodes.content: Intersection (A ∩ B) is elements in both; Union (A ∪ B) is elements in either.

nodes.title: Unit 2: Functions
nodes.content: Definitions of domain, target, range, and well-definedness.

nodes.title: Function Definition
nodes.content: A mapping f: X -> Y where every x in X relates to exactly one y in Y.

nodes.title: Well-defined Functions
nodes.content: A function is well-defined if every domain element maps to exactly one (not zero or multiple) target element.

nodes.title: Range
nodes.content: The subset of the target Y consisting of elements actually mapped to by the domain X.

nodes.title: Unit 3: Mathematical Definitions
nodes.content: Standard definitions for parity, rationality, and number theory.

nodes.title: Even and Odd (Parity)
nodes.content: Even: x = 2k. Odd: x = 2k + 1. Integers must be one or the other.

nodes.title: Rational Numbers
nodes.content: Numbers expressible as a/b where a, b are integers and b != 0.

nodes.title: Divisibility
nodes.content: x divides y if y = kx for some integer k.

nodes.title: Prime and Composite
nodes.content: Prime: n > 1 divisible only by 1 and n. Composite: n > 1 with a factor m where 1 < m < n.

nodes.title: Unit 4: Proof Techniques
nodes.content: Methods for proving universal and existential statements and avoiding common errors.

nodes.title: Universal Generalization
nodes.content: Proving a statement for an arbitrary element to conclude it holds for the entire domain.

nodes.title: Proof by Exhaustion
nodes.content: Proving a statement by testing every single element in a finite, small domain.

nodes.title: Existence Proofs
nodes.content: Constructive (finds/builds example) or Nonconstructive (proves existence without an example).

nodes.title: Existential Instantiation
nodes.content: Logic law: if an object exists, it can be named for the purpose of a proof.

nodes.title: Counterexamples
nodes.content: A single specific instance that proves a universal statement is false.
